It was another great week: Matt Belloni assessed an Oscars unforced error; =

an=E2=80=99s latest gamble; Marion Maneker chatted with the Christie=E2=80=
=99s chairman; Julie Davich compiled a midseason auction report; and Baratu=
nde Thurston
conducted the second quarterly edition of The Puck Private Conversation, po=
wered by Orchestra.=C2=A0

=C2=A0

Meanwhile, Julia Ioffe conveyed the fallacy of Trump=E2=80=99s Putinphilia;=
 Leigh Ann Caldwell broke the news of the president=E2=80=99s House interve=
ntion; Peter Hamby detailed Elon=E2=80=99s Icarus moment; and Tara Palmeri
plumbed some Biden regrets.

=C2=A0

Check out these stories, and others, via the links below. And stick around =
for the backstory on how it all came together.

=C2=A0

P.S.: I=E2=80=99d like to quickly correct an error in last night=E2=80=99s =
issue of In the Room. In an item, we confused outgoing Washington Post opin=
ion
editor David Shipley with New York Times opinion editorial director David L=
eonhardt. Shipley is not going to the Times. (Although, to be honest, I wou=
ldn=E2=80=99t be surprised if he ended up there one day.) It was an honest =
mistake and we regret the error.

And the Winner Is=E2=80=A6
--------------------

Unlike most people who get into the media
business, I didn=E2=80=99t start out in a newsroom or the mailroom; I wasn=
=E2=80=99t a producer=E2=80=99s aide, or an ad planner or marketing intern.=
 Instead, I was a glorified manservant at golden-age-era Vanity Fair=E2=80=
=94at one point, an assistant to an assistant whose responsibilities includ=
ed fetching Pluck U Chicken when the junior editors had hangovers; more tha=
n occasionally retrieving an executive=E2=80=99s dress from Madame Paulette=
 dry cleaners in Midtown; ordering Christopher Hitchens scotch for
his birthday (and Dominick Dunne flowers on his); and taking the A train to=
 Canal Street to burn For Your Consideration DVDs of Oscar-contending movie=
s, distributed by the studios in advance of the Academy Awards.

=C2=A0

In those days, of course, the biological clock of the joint revolved around=
 the Oscars. My boss, the legendary editor Graydon
Carter, threw the party of the year around the annual ceremony. His was suc=
h a coveted invite that the events team would blow out a wall at Morton=E2=
=80=99s on Melrose to build a veritable banquet hall atop the parking lot t=
o fit everyone. Meanwhile, guests were invited to enter during specific tim=
e windows, both to avoid overcrowding=E2=80=94L.A. county officials were co=
nsulted during the planning, of course=E2=80=94but also to subtly reflect t=
heir status. The real swells, like Geffen and
Mick Jagger, might arrive to watch the award show at dinner. The riffraff w=
as allowed in after midnight. Sara Marks, whom some of us half-joked would =
have been equipped to lead Operation Iraqi Freedom, manned the guest list f=
rom the chauffeur drop-off. Crashers, and Courtney Love, were bounced.

=C2=A0

All these decades later, Oscar weekend remains my Proustian
madeleine. Of course, the industry has changed profoundly in the intervenin=
g years as streaming, cord-cutting, the rise of the trillion-dollar tech pl=
atforms and other secular forces have reshaped how audiences consume movies=
=2E I remember walking through the VF p=
arty back in 2005 when Million Dollar=20=
Baby, the definitionally monocultural $200 million grosser starring Hilary =
Swank and directed by the great Clint Eastwood, won best picture. Ever
since, as our culture has balkanized, Oscar favorites have become increasin=
gly niche-ified. It=E2=80=99s just the way media works these days.=C2=A0

=C2=A0

In his latest piece, my partner Matt Belloni offers an incredibly insightfu=
l analysis of one of the latent factors in the transformation=E2=80=94a gro=
wing split between what American moviegoers watch on the big screen and the=
 films that take home gold
statuettes. During the past decade, as the Academy rightly attempted to div=
ersify its ranks, around 40 percent of the new invites went to members outs=
ide the U.S. And while this sort of cosmopolitanism was overdue, in some re=
spects, it also shaped the Academy=E2=80=99s taste, thereby impacting which=
 movies got nominated and eventually won awards. =E2=80=9CThis isn=E2=80=99=
t an artistic point, or even a complaint about the =E2=80=98qualifications=
=E2=80=99 of these international invites,=E2=80=9D Matt noted astutely. =E2=
=80=9CBut when voters have no
connection to the Hollywood film industry=E2=80=94or, in some cases, even a=
n interest in the kinds of movies that are shown in U.S. theaters=E2=80=94h=
ow can the Academy expect them to nominate movies that appeal to the Americ=
an audience that consumes mostly Hollywood films and might want to watch th=
e Oscars on a Sunday night?=E2=80=9D Matt offers a fascinating depiction of=
 the dynamic in The
Academy Did This to Itself ( https://puck.news/how-and-why-the-academys-tas=
tes-have-changed/?utm_campaign=3DThe+Backstory+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%283%2F1%2F25%=
29&utm_content=3DThe+Backstory+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%283%2F1%2F25%29&utm_medium=3D=
email_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a ).

=C2=A0

Tastes do change, of course. (Although, I have to say, for some reason I re=
cently found myself rewatching Hannah and Her Sisters, a 1987 best picture =
nominee, and it still holds up perfectly.) And the fashion industry knows m=
ore about capriciousness than any other market we cover here at Puck. If yo=
u have time to read only one piece this weekend, I=E2=80=99d turn your atte=
ntion to
Lauren Sherman=E2=80=99s fantastic Gucci Successorology & Burberry=E2=80=99=
s Mini Revival ( https://puck.news/daniel-lees-burberry-triumph-and-guccis-=
next-chapter/?utm_campaign=3DThe+Backstory+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%283%2F1%2F25%29&u=
tm_content=3DThe+Backstory+-+SUBSCRIBERS+%283%2F1%2F25%29&utm_medium=3Demai=
l_action&utm_source=3Dcustomer.io&utm_term=3Df6c60617da42abd12a ). Indeed, =
the industry=E2=80=99s biggest brands are enduring their own quiet evolutio=
n, too, away from singular star creative directors and in pursuit of safer,=
 annual-recurring-revenue-friendly strategies that make them less immune to=
 key man risk. The business of art and
the art of business remain the story of our time, and something you should =
always expect to read about in Puck.

=E2=80=8A

<table class=3D"paragraph_block block-4" width=3D"100%" border=3D"0" cellpa=
dding=3D"0" cellspacing=3D"0" role=3D"presentation" style=3D"mso-table-lspa=
ce:0pt;mso-table-rspace:0pt;word-break:break-word;"><tbody><tr><td class=3D=
"pad" style=3D"padding-bottom:32px;"><div style=3D"color:#000000;direction:=
ltr;font-family:'Inter','Arial';font-size:17px;font-weight:=
400;letter-spacing:0px;line-height:150%;text-align:left;mso-line-height-alt=
:25.5px;"><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">Unlike most people who g=
et into the media
business, I didn=E2=80=99t start out in a newsroom or the mailroom; I wasn=
=E2=80=99t a producer=E2=80=99s aide, or an ad planner or marketing intern.=
 Instead, I was a glorified manservant at golden-age-era <em>Vanity Fair</e=
m>=E2=80=94at one point, an assistant to an assistant whose responsibilitie=
s included fetching Pluck U Chicken when the junior editors had hangovers; =
more than occasionally retrieving an executive=E2=80=99s dress from Madame =
Paulette dry cleaners in Midtown; ordering <strong>Christopher Hitchens </s=
trong>scotch for
his birthday (and <strong>Dominick Dunne </strong>flowers<strong> </strong>=
on his); and taking the A train to Canal Street to burn <em>For Your</em> <=
em>Consideration</em> DVDs of Oscar-contending movies, distributed by the s=
tudios in advance of the Academy Awards.</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bot=
tom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">In those days,=
 of course, the biological clock of the joint revolved around the Oscars. M=
y boss, the legendary editor <strong>Graydon
Carter</strong>, threw the party of the year around the annual ceremony. Hi=
s was such a coveted invite that the events team would blow out a wall at M=
orton=E2=80=99s on Melrose to build a veritable banquet hall atop the parki=
ng lot to fit everyone. Meanwhile, guests were invited to enter during spec=
ific time windows, both to avoid overcrowding=E2=80=94L.A. county officials=
 were consulted during the planning, of course=E2=80=94but also to subtly r=
eflect their status. The real swells, like <strong>Geffen </strong>and
<strong>Mick Jagger</strong>, might arrive to watch the award show at dinne=
r. The riffraff was allowed in after midnight. <strong>Sara Marks</strong>,=
 whom some of us half-joked would have been equipped to lead Operation Iraq=
i Freedom, manned the guest list from the chauffeur drop-off. Crashers, and=
 <strong>Courtney Love</strong>, were bounced.</p><p style=3D"margin:0;marg=
in-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">All thes=
e decades later, Oscar weekend remains my Proustian
madeleine. Of course, the industry has changed profoundly in the intervenin=
g years as streaming, cord-cutting, the rise of the trillion-dollar tech pl=
atforms and other secular forces have reshaped how audiences consume movies=
=2E I remember walking through the <em>=
VF </em>party back in 2005 when <em>Mi=
llion Dollar Baby</em>, the definitionally monocultural $200 million grosse=
r starring <strong>Hilary Swank </strong>and directed by the great <strong>=
Clint Eastwood</strong>, won best picture. Ever
since, as our culture has balkanized, Oscar favorites have become increasin=
gly niche-ified. It=E2=80=99s just the way media works these days.=C2=A0</p=
><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;ma=
rgin-bottom:0px;">In his latest piece, my partner <strong>Matt Belloni </st=
rong>offers an incredibly insightful analysis of one of the latent factors =
in the transformation=E2=80=94a growing split between what American moviego=
ers watch on the big screen and the films that take home gold
statuettes. During the past decade, as the Academy rightly attempted to div=
ersify its ranks, around 40 percent of the new invites went to members outs=
ide the U.S. And while this sort of cosmopolitanism was overdue, in some re=
spects, it also shaped the Academy=E2=80=99s taste, thereby impacting which=
 movies got nominated and eventually won awards. =E2=80=9CThis isn=E2=80=99=
t an artistic point, or even a complaint about the =E2=80=98qualifications=
=E2=80=99 of these international invites,=E2=80=9D Matt noted astutely. =E2=
=80=9CBut when voters have no
connection to the Hollywood film industry=E2=80=94or, in some cases, even a=
n interest in the kinds of movies that are shown in U.S. theaters=E2=80=94h=
ow can the Academy expect them to nominate movies that appeal to the Americ=
an audience that consumes mostly Hollywood films and might want to watch th=
e Oscars on a Sunday night?=E2=80=9D Matt offers a fascinating depiction of=
 the dynamic in <a href=3D"https://email.puck.news/e/c/eyJlbWFpbF9pZCI6ImRn=
VDJ4Z1lEQUt2UktxclJLZ0dWVWZRZmk5UEFwajQxWFRpOEo2QT0iLCJocmVmIjoiaHR0cHM6Ly9=
wdWNrLm5ld3MvaG93LWFuZC13aHktdGhlLWFjYWRlbXlzLXRhc3Rlcy1oYXZlLWNoYW5nZWQvP3=
V0bV9jYW1wYWlnbj1UaGUrQmFja3N0b3J5Ky0rU1VCU0NSSUJFUlMrJTI4MyUyRjElMkYyNSUyO=
Vx1MDAyNnV0bV9jb250ZW50PVRoZStCYWNrc3RvcnkrLStTVUJTQ1JJQkVSUyslMjgzJTJGMSUy=
RjI1JTI5XHUwMDI2dXRtX21lZGl1bT1lbWFpbF9hY3Rpb25cdTAwMjZ1dG1fc291cmNlPWN1c3R=
vbWVyLmlvXHUwMDI2dXRtX3Rlcm09ZjZjNjA2MTdkYTQyYWJkMTJhIiwiaW50ZXJuYWwiOiJmNm=
M2MDYxN2RhNDJhYmQxMmEiLCJsaW5rX2lkIjoyMDk2NjI0fQ/794565e2da4610d77f0c635a99=
babf3523a141d8c368be2c892878240e89a871" style=3D"text-decoration:underline;=
color:#d40000;"><em>The
Academy Did This to Itself</em></a>.</p><p style=3D"margin:0;margin-bottom:=
0px;">=C2=A0</p><p style=3D"margin:0;">Tastes do change, of course. (Althou=
gh, I have to say, for some reason I recently found myself rewatching <em>H=
annah and Her Sisters</em>, a 1987 best picture nominee, and it still holds=
 up perfectly.) And the fashion industry knows more about capriciousness th=
an any other market we cover here at Puck. If you have time to read only on=
e piece this weekend, I=E2=80=99d turn your attention to
<strong>Lauren Sherman</strong>=E2=80=99s fantastic <a href=3D"https://emai=
l.puck.news/e/c/eyJlbWFpbF9pZCI6ImRnVDJ4Z1lEQUt2UktxclJLZ0dWVWZRZmk5UEFwajQ=
xWFRpOEo2QT0iLCJocmVmIjoiaHR0cHM6Ly9wdWNrLm5ld3MvZGFuaWVsLWxlZXMtYnVyYmVycn=
ktdHJpdW1waC1hbmQtZ3VjY2lzLW5leHQtY2hhcHRlci8_dXRtX2NhbXBhaWduPVRoZStCYWNrc=
3RvcnkrLStTVUJTQ1JJQkVSUyslMjgzJTJGMSUyRjI1JTI5XHUwMDI2dXRtX2NvbnRlbnQ9VGhl=
K0JhY2tzdG9yeSstK1NVQlNDUklCRVJTKyUyODMlMkYxJTJGMjUlMjlcdTAwMjZ1dG1fbWVkaXV=
tPWVtYWlsX2FjdGlvblx1MDAyNnV0bV9zb3VyY2U9Y3VzdG9tZXIuaW9cdTAwMjZ1dG1fdGVybT=
1mNmM2MDYxN2RhNDJhYmQxMmEiLCJpbnRlcm5hbCI6ImY2YzYwNjE3ZGE0MmFiZDEyYSIsImxpb=
mtfaWQiOjIwOTY0MTN9/d45834876ab36b6d0d74c4e027a31a258219531f9019452016d2a33=
f736fdf61" style=3D"text-decoration:underline;color:#d40000;"><em>Gucci Suc=
cessorology & Burberry=E2=80=99s Mini Revival</em></a>. Indeed, the ind=
ustry=E2=80=99s biggest brands are enduring their own quiet evolution, too,=
 away from singular star creative directors and in pursuit of safer, annual=
-recurring-revenue-friendly strategies that make them less immune to key ma=
n risk. The business of art and
the art of business remain the story of our time, and something you should =
always expect to read about in Puck.</p></div>
